hook: Rock carvings in this far off region attest to the spread of Krishna tradition widely. Balarama and Krishna are seen in these rock carvings. Where is this found?
explanation: Rock carvings at Chilas in Kashmir, along a major trade route in the Gilgit valley, include a pair of figures, holding what seem to be a plough and disc, wearing wide open coats similar to that of the Kushana period. They are identified as Balarama and Krishna based on the Kharoshthi inscriptions found in the area. Source: A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India From the Stone Age to the 12th Century by Upinder Singh

hook: This Greek Ambassador to India erected a stone pillar venerating Lord Krishna. This is one among the earliest surviving Hindu religious structures in India. Remains of a temple too have been found in front of this pillar. In which modern State of India will you find this pillar still standing?
explanation: Heliodorus, the Greek ambassador, describes himself as a Bhagavata—i.e., a worshipper of the god Vasudeva Krishna, and that he set up this pillar in honor of this god. The pillar is dated around 120 BCE. Nearby the remains of an ancient temple dated to the 3rd Century BCE have been found. The pillar is still standing in Besnagar, near Vidisha in Madhya Pradesh. Source: A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India From the Stone Age to the 12th Century by Upinder Singh

hook: As Krishna was being carried from Mathura in a cart to escape Mughal destruction, his deity got stuck in this place and is now a popular pilgrimage spot. What is this place called?
explanation: Shrinathji’s idol was brought to Rajasthan from Govardhana near Vrindavan to protect it from the hands of the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb who in 1665 was bent upon vandalizing the area of Vrindavan by widespread destruction of Hindu temples. For almost six months the statue stayed in Agra after which the custodians of the idol of Shrinathji left that place with the idol in search of a new location. When the wheel of the chariot carrying the Lord got stuck in the mud at a place called Sihar, the Maharana Rajsingh of Mewar saw it as a divine sign that Lord Krishna wished to settle here, and thus a temple was built at this spot and the holy township of Nathdwara grew around the temple._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.nathdwaratemple.org/history/shrinathjis-arrival-at-nathdwara

hook: Somnath, a Jyotirling is also closely connected to Krishna's life, how?
explanation: Bhalka Teerth is the location where the arrow of the poacher Jara hit Sri Krishna. It is about 4 or 5 kms away from Somnath. Bhagvan Shree Krishna was resting in meditation pose under a pipal tree when the poacher misread the foot of Bhagvan Shree Krishna as a deer and hit from a distance. This divined leela of Bhagvan Shree Krishna is immortalized by a beautiful temple and an ancient pipal tree. Bhagvan Shree Krishna then walked a small distance and arrived at the holy banks of river Hiran from where he took his last journey to his Neejdham. Source: Somnath Trust

hook: What is the material from which the deities in Puri Jagannath temple are made of ?
explanation: The deities are made of wood. The deities are replaced at intervals of 8, 11, 12 or 19 years, most often after 19 years. The ritual is called Nabakalebara. Four holy neem trees are identified and cut to make the famous wooden embodiments of Jagannātha Krishna. The goddess Mangalā is the guardian of the forest from which trees are cut. The priests come to the forest to seek the right tree for the new image. Source: Immortal India Volume 1, https://www.shreejagannatha.in/nabakalebara/

hook: Goddess Vimala is a Shakti Peeth and is housed within a famous Krishna temple. Where is Goddess Vimala?
explanation: At the famous pilgrimage temple of Krishna Jagannātha at Purī in Orissa, the goddess Vimalā is known as the ancient goddess of the place. Those who have studied the folk deities of Orissa and the emergence of the Krishna worship here say that this place, too, was a pitha of the Goddess long before being appropriated by Lord Krishna. Nīlāchala, on which the temple sits today, was once the domain of the goddess named Vimalā. Her shrine still is present in the sacred precincts, and she is the first to receive the prasād, the consecrated food offerings that only then become the mahāprasād, the “great grace of the Lord,” to be distributed to the worshippers. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Seva Kunj in Vrindavan is the location of one of the Leelas of Krishna.
explanation: Among the most famous of the līlāsthalas near the Yamunā bank are the forest groves where Krishna would meet the gopīs at night and dance the rās dance with each of them in a wide circle. He created a well for them when they were exhausted with the dance and too thirsty to walk to the Yamunā River. There, too, is Sevā Kunj, the very bower, so they say, where Krishna met Rādhā for lovemaking. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: The site called Gopishvara in Vrindavan marks Shiva in the life of Krishna. Which incident is this
explanation: Shiva himself became a gopī to witness and enjoy the sacred rās dance of Krishna with the gopīs. He was drawn to Braj by the sound of Krishna’s flute, but was refused entry and could not cross the Yamunā until he became a woman. So Shiva bathed in the river and emerged a gopī. At Gopīshvara in the evening, Shiva is adorned by the priests with a bright sari and the silver mask of a woman wearing lipstick and eye makeup. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Pushti Marg, the Path of Grace, is a tradition of Krishna worship. What aspect of Krishna is worshipped?
explanation: The Pushti Mārg tradition of Krishna worship creates yet another landscape—a linked network of havelīs, each housing a small and powerful image of Krishna, and a network of baithaks where Vallabha taught. At any one of these havelīs, the pilgrim experience brings vicarious participation as members of the extended family in the daily life of Krishna—his waking and sleeping, his caring for the cows and coming home at dusk, his swinging and his water sports. Visiting a havelī, one will catch the Lord somewhere in the daily domestic cycle and enter into that very moment._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Krishna in this temple turned 180 degrees to allow his disciple Kanakadasa his glimpse since he was not allowed into this temple. Where is this temple?
explanation: A devotee named Kanakadāsa came as a pilgrim to Udupi but was unable to enter the temple because of his station in life as a shepherd. So he sang songs of devotion outside the temple, and Krishna, they say, was moved by his song. The Lord opened up a crack in the temple wall and turned 180 degrees to face west so that Kanakadāsa could have his darshan. Rather than patch up the crack, the temple authorities turned it into a window, which they dubbed Kanakadāsa’s window, through which everyone can peep in from the outside and see Lord Krishna. Today, it is customary to peek through Kanakadāsa’s silver-framed and highly ornate window before entering the temple for darshan. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Rinchor in Dakor, close to Ahmedabad is a Krishna temple. What is the origin of the deity?
explanation: An aging devotee used to make the trek from Dakor to Dvāraka on a regular basis to honor Krishna with tulsī leaves he had grown himself. Eventually, he became too old and lame to do so, and so he drove to Dvārakā in a cart intending to seize Krishna and take the image home with him to Dakor. Despite the security precautions of the Dvārakā Temple, Krishna was willingly kidnapped and spirited away in the cart to Dakor, where he is honored today as Rinchor. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: A gigantic boulder precariously positioned is known as Lord Krishna’s butter ball. Where will you find this?
explanation: Mahabalipuram is a UNESCO heritage site developed by the Pallava dynasty in the 7th and 8th centuries. Several rock cut temples, cave sanctuaries and rock carvings dot this area. The Krishna's Butter Ball appears precariously tilted, but has defined several attempts to move it. Source:

hook: Which Krishna temple is called the Dwaraka of the South
explanation: The Lord Krishna asked his foremost disciple and devotee, Uddhava to retrieve the idol from the impending submergence of Dwaraka in the sea and install it at a spot equally holy so that He could shower His blessings on His devotees and save them from the evil effects of Kaliyuga. Uddhava entrusted the job to Guru, the preceptor of the gods, and Vayu, the god of winds. They took possession of the idol and moved in search of a proper place. Ultimately they decided to install it near a lake full of lotuses, on one side of which there were Siva and Parvati. That is Guruvayur, the place having derived its name from Guru and Vayu who jointly installed the vigraha. Source: Temples and Legends of Kerala by Bharatiya Bhavan

hook: In what form does Krishna appear in Guruvayoor
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ The deity in Guruvayur represents the full manifestation of the Lord Mahavishnu as was revealed to Vasudeva and Devaki at the time of Krishnavatara; form of Sri Krishna endowed with the four lustrous arms carrying the conch, the discus, the mace and the lotus._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Temples and Legends of Kerala by Bharatiya Bhavan

hook: In one of the Krishna temples in Kerala, Tiruchambaram, an animal is banned and is also not supposed to be brought near the temple. Which animal is it?
explanation: Tiruchambaram is associated with Kamsavadha. Here the jubilant Krishna after the killing of Kamsa is depicted in all his splendor at Tiruchambaram. Kamsa had arranged for the Kuvalayapida, the royal elephant to encounter Krishna and Balarama as they marched towards Kamsa. Krishna fought with the elephant and killed it. Therefore elephants are not taken near the temple although elephants are very much part of all other Kerala temples. The belief is also that Krishna, after killing Kamsa went to his mother Devaki and asked for food stating that he was very hungry after the ordeal. Therefore, unlike in other temples, Naivedya is offered as soon as the temple is opened. Source: Temples and Legends of Kerala by Bharatiya Bhavan

hook: Chappan Bhog, the feast of 56 items, has its origins in one of the Krishna Leelas. Which one is it?
explanation: Krishna sheltered the villagers of Gokul under the GovardhanGiri for seven days and seven nights. During this entire period, he did not eat and was focused on protecting his people. Normally he is fed eight times a day. Therefore 56 items were prepared to quench his hunger after the Leela representing each of the meals he had missed. Source: Bhoga Naivedyam by Sujata Shukla

hook: In which Indian city are you likely to find a famous temple for Krishna as the Charioteer of Arjuna
explanation: The Parthasarathy temple in Chennai is more than 1500 years old. Here Krishna is manifest as the Charioteer of Arjuna and is depicted with a moustache and with a conch in his hand. His face is full of arrow marks, representing the arrows that he stopped during the war. Source:

hook: Agathacoles coins refer to set of die struck coins issued by Indo Greek king Agathocles. These have one of the earliest images of Krishna and Balarama. Where were these coins found?
explanation: These coins were found in Al Khanoum in Afghanistan. Both sides of the coins bear images of male figures. What is most striking about these figures are the attributes that differentiate them. The figure on the obverse holds a miniature plough in his left hand, identifying him as Balarama, who is also known as Haladhara (wielder of the plough). In his right hand, he holds up a pestle (musala). The figure on the reverse holds a large, six-spoked wheel in his left hand against his left flank, which makes it look like a shield. This wheel (chakra) is the symbol of Vasudeva Krishna. In his right hand, he holds up something which may be a conch shell. The figure on the obverse stands between the two lines of a vertical legend in Greek, giving the name of king Agathocles. The iconographic details make the identification of the two figures certain, not ambiguous. These coins have been dated with precision to the reign of Agathocles, c. 180–170 BCE. Source: A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India From the Stone Age to the 12th Century by Upinder Singh

hook: Tens of thousands of people walk every year to this Krishna pilgrimage site. Which is this?
explanation: Pandharpur, west of Solapur in Maharashtra, is one of the major pilgrimage sites. Vithoba or Vithala or Panduranga is worshipped in Pandharpur. Vithoba is a form of Krishna. His consort is Rukmini. Pandharpur Vari is the yatra of Varkaris the followers of saints of Maharashtra, notably, Jnaneesvar and Tukaram. Every year, they reach Pandharpur on the Ekadashi of the Ashadha month carrying Palkhis. The main Palkhis start from Alandi and Dehu and meet in Pune. Palkhis of several other saints also join the pilgrimage. They reach Pandharpur on the Ashadha Shukla 10th. The Varis have been a continuous tradition for several centuries and had united the masses. Source: Immortal India Volume 1 by Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan.

hook: Yamuna is curved in one place along its course and it is supposed to be due to Balarama dragging the river with his plough. Where is this location?
explanation: This is in Vrindavan. In one story, Balarama was mocked by Yamuna for trying to imitate Krishna's dances crudely. In a rage, Balarama drew the river with a deep furrow of his plough. An another version, Balarama got intoxicated and desired to take a bath. He ordered the river to come near him and on her refusal to come, he dragged her. Source: Immortal India Volume 2 by Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan.

hook: India’s national calendar is based on an era that some traditions say honors a Satavahana ruler, the first of the Deccan dynasties. Which is this calendar?
explanation: The national calendar, based on the Saka Era, was adopted on March 22, 1957, along with the Gregorian calendar for official use. The Saka era started in 78 CE. Historians disagree on the events it marks. A later tradition (after the 13th century CE) suggests the Saka era marks King Shalivahana's reign. The Satavahana ruler Gautamiputra Satkarni defeated the Saka satraps of Western India, who were of Iranian origin. This was considered an important event. Other traditions say the Saka era celebrates the Saka rulers. Satavahanas (also called Shalivahanas) are known as Andhras in the Puranas. They were governors of the Deccan under the Mauryas and became independent in the 1st century BCE. They ruled for 300 years and fought against the Sakas, who were invading India. The Sakas outlived the Satavahanas but were eventually defeated by the Guptas. Under the Satavahanas, the Konkan coast became a major trading area. A 1st-century CE book by an Egyptian merchant mentions Chaul (Vasai), Sopara, Kalyan, and Gharapuri (Elephanta) as important ports on the Konkan coast. Although they were Saivites, the Satavahanas supported all religious groups. The Konkan area became a stronghold of Buddhism. During their rule, some famous Buddhist cave temples, such as the Karla Caves, were built. Source: RC Majumdar, “Ancient India”; AS Altekar, writing on Satavahanas in the Maharashtra State Gazetteer. The picture is Satavahana architecture at Pandavleni Caves at Nashik, during the 2nd century BCE.

hook: Queen Prabhavati, a Vakataka, was one of the first women to hold power over a large kingdom. Which famous dynasty was she born into?
explanation: The Vakatakas succeeded the Satavahanas. They ruled parts of the Deccan from the mid-3rd to the late 5th century CE, from Nandivardhana, near Nagpur. Their rule overlapped with the Guptas to the north. Chandragupta II gave his daughter Prabhavati Gupta in marriage to the Vakataka ruler Rudrasena II. This was likely to gain Vakataka support for his campaign against the Sakas in Gujarat and Malwa._x000D_ _x000D_ After her husband’s death, Prabhavati became regent for her three young sons and later ruled directly for two decades (390-410 CE). The Pune Copper Plate inscription of Prabhavati Gupta mentions the year she became regent. It also identifies her gotra as her father’s dynasty, not her husband’s, and gives details about her father and her mother, who was a Naga princess. This inscription shows that she ruled independently._x000D_ _x000D_ Because of her regency, the Guptas and Vakatakas became allies, bringing political unity to a large part of India. Prabhavati is considered the original philosopher queen of India. It is said that Kalidasa, the great Sanskrit poet, was in her court._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Priya Jyoti Samanta, ‘Poona Copper Plate Inscription of Prabhavati Gupta’, Sadvidya - Journal of Research in Sanskrit; RC Majumdar, “Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ A painting of Prabhavatigupta by Asit Kumar Haldar

hook: The Gangas commissioned one of the greatest monuments of Jainism at Shrvanabelagola in Karnataka. What is the monument called?
explanation: Shravanabelagola is an important Jain pilgrimage site in South Karnataka. It has an 18-meter-high statue of Lord Gometeshwara Bahubali. It is considered one of the world’s tallest free-standing monoliths. Chamundaraya, a minister and general of Rachamalla IV of the Ganga dynasty, built it in 981 AD. The statue is carved from a single block of granite, on a hilltop, and is visible from up to 30 km away. Nearly 700 steps are cut into the rock to reach the statue. Chandragupta Maurya is believed to have spent his final days here after giving up his throne, though this is debated. He was accompanied by his guru, Bhadrabahu. The Western Gangas ruled in Mysore state (Gangavadi) from about 250 to about 1004 CE. They maintained their power through marriages and military alliances with the Pallavas, Chalukyas, and Kadambas. They ruled between the Tungabhadra and Krishna rivers, from Talakad (the capital) to Vatapi. The Cholas conquered them around 1004. Most of the Western Gangas were Jains, but some supported Brahmanical Hinduism. They supported Kannada scholarship, built notable temples, and promoted deforestation, irrigation, farming, and trade across the peninsula. Shravanabelagola and its surroundings have many Jain bastis (of the Digambara sect). Source: Karnataka Tourism, Brittanica. The picture is the deity of Gometeshwara Bahubali.

hook: Like Birbal was to Akbar, Tenali Rama was to which Vijayanagara king?
explanation: Tenali Ramakrishna was a 16th-century CE Telugu poet in the court of Krishnadevaraya, the Vijayanagara ruler. He was one of the eight prominent poets (astadiggajas) patronized by the court. He wrote several religious works. His work, Panduranga Mahatmya, is considered a landmark in Telugu kavyas. He was also called ‘vikatakavi’ (jester poet) and was known for blending wisdom with humor. Like Birbal, Tenali Rama is known for folktales where he used his wit to solve problems. However, there are no historical sources for these stories. From the mid-14th to the mid-16th century CE, the Vijayanagara empire influenced the culture south of the Vindhyas. They supported the temple at Tirupati, which is still one of the most visited temples in India. Statues of Krishnadevaraya and his two wives are in one of the temple's mandapas. The Dussehra celebrations at Mysuru are a Vijayanagara tradition still followed by the Wodeyar royal family, who were feudatories to the empire. The Vijayanagara empire ended after a defeat by the combined forces of the five Deccan sultanates in 1565 CE. The ruins of their capital, Hampi, show the empire's greatness. Source: MAP Academy The picture is Tenali Rama’s statue at Tenali

hook: The Yadavas of Devagiri fought the Delhi sultans for three generations. Their defeat allowed Muslim rule in the Deccan. What is the modern name of Devagiri?
explanation: Like the Hoysalas, the Yadavas of Devagiri (now Daulatabad) claimed to be descendants of Krishna. Both rose to power in the 10th century CE when the Rashtrakuta and Chalukya power declined. The Yadavas ruled in Maharashtra, and the Hoysalas ruled in Karnataka. They often fought each other, despite their claimed kinship. They also fought with the Kakatiyas of Andhra. The three dynasties did not unite against the Sultans and were defeated one by one. The Yadavas were the first to be defeated. Alauddin Khilji defeated Ramachandra Yadav in 1296 CE. Ramachandra took refuge in his fort at Devagiri, which was thought to be impenetrable. However, due to mismanagement of food supplies, he was forced to surrender. A few years later, he refused to pay tribute. Malik Kafur defeated him again in 1307 CE. Ramachandra accepted vassalship, but his son Sankara refused and was killed in a war with Kafur. Ramachandra’s son-in-law Harapala continued the fight but was also defeated, captured, and killed. The Yadava resistance ended, and the Deccan came under Muslim rule. Source: RC Majumdar, “Ancient India” The picture is Devagiri Fort

hook: Kempa Gowda is considered as the founder of Bengaluru. He was the governor of which kingdom?
explanation: Kempa Gowda was born in Yelahanka, now a suburb of Bengaluru. His family were vassals of Yelehanka for the Vijayanagara empire. In 1537, he erected a mud fort and started a township within it with the permission of the Vijayanagara emperor. He called it the Bengaluru Fort. Contrary to popular stories about the name Bengaluru being linked to Brinjal or Bengal gram, historical inscriptions refer to Bengaluru from 1200 years back. The fort consisted of eight gates, referring to now popular areas such as Halsoor, Anekal, Kengeri and Yelehanka. He shifted his capital from Yelehanka to Bengaluru. Numerous “pete” areas came up within the fort dedicated to particular professions and goods. The fort is near the areas of Nagarathpet and Chikpet. Kempa Gowda made the mistake of minting his own coin which infuriated the Vijayanagar emperor. Kempa Gowda was imprisoned for five years as a result. The fort was improved by Tipu Sultan and was eventually brought down the British. Shivaji’s father obtained a Jagir for the Fort and surrounding areas from the Bijapur sultanate. The area passed on to Shivaji’s stepbrother who then lost the Fort to Aurangzeb’s army. They later on sold it to the Wodeyar’s of Mysore. Source: Fazlul Hasan, “Bangalore through the Centuries.” The picture is of the remains of Bangalore Fort

hook: INS Kadamba is an important naval base of India Navy celebrating a famous dynasty. Where is INS Kadamba?
explanation: Kadambas were a minor dynastic power that held sway in coastal Karnataka between 4th-6th cent CE. Their chroniclers claim that the family migrated from northern India, but other records suggest that they were indigenous to Kuntala (northern Kanara). An early inscription, the accuracy of which is unknown, describes the dynastic founder, Mayurasharman. He was a learned Brahman who was insulted by a Pallava official. He took up a military career and acquired sufficient territory to bargain with the Pallavas for a feudal principality on the western coast. Pulakesi II, the warlike Chalukya captured their capital Banavasi in the mid 7th cent CE and their rule ended. They reappear in historical records as the rulers of Goa between 10th-14th cent CE, having defeated the Shilaharas. The Kadambas have left a more lasting impact on Goan history. The bus service in Goa is named after them. Many old houses in Goa still sport the lion emblem of the Kadambas. The Indian navy named one of its bases INS Kadamba. Source: Britannica The picture is of INS Kadamba at Karwar

hook: The Hoysala temples have the most exquisite carvings as they used a softer stone. Which stone is this?
explanation: Hoysalas ruled southern Karnataka, mainly the Hassan, Mysore, Shimoga, Chitradurga districts. Their reign lasted between the 10th and 14th centuries. They extended their influence southwards into the areas ruled by Chola and Pandyan kings. Hoysalas are renowned for their temple architecture. They used soap stone for their intricate carvings. Soapstone has a high talc (magnesium) content, making it easy to carve. The Hoysala temples display a unique artistic style and extraordinary intricacy of carving. The Hoysala temples at Belur, Halabeidu and Somanathapura are UNESCO heritage sites. There are several hundred Hoysala temples in southern Karnataka. While they have been damaged extensively in invasions, the remains are still magnificent. Source: William Coehlo, Hoysala Vamsa The picture is from Hoysaleswara temple of Halabeidu

hook: Which wondrous temple, carved from a single rock, will forever be associated with the greatness of the Rashtrakutas?
explanation: Cave Number 16 of Ellora is the Kailasanatha temple. It represents an audacity of imagination like none other. It is a massive monolithic rock cut temple. There is nothing like this anywhere else in India or the world. Bulk of the carving of the temple was completed under the patronage of Krishna I of the Rashtrakuta dynasty. The Kailasanatha temple at Ellora is influenced by the Virupaksha temple at Pattadakal. Krishna I had defeated the Badami Chalukyas who had constructed the Virupaksha temple. It in turn was inspired by the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram built by Pallavas. The Chalukyas had defeated the Pallavas in battle. Thus, the rock cut temple of Ellora traces its origin across three empires. Ellora’s Kailasanatha was earlier known as Manikeshwar and Ghrishneshwar and was believed to be one of the 12 Jyotirlingas. At present, the Ghrishneshwar temple built by Holkars in Verul, adjacent to Ellora, is worshipped as a Jyotirlinga. Rashtrakutas reigned from the 6th to 10th century, but were at their peak in the 8th to 10th centuries. Their capital was at Manyakheta, a famous city of antiquity. At some point or other, they defeated the great powers of the time such as the Pratiharas, Palas, Eastern Chalukyas, and Cholas. They had invaded Kannauj. Their greatest king was Amoghavarsha who is said to have turned Jain in later life. A muslim traveler called him among the four greatest monarchs of the world. Like most other dynasties, the Rashtrakutas were ecletic in their patronage to sects. They were also instrumental in encouraging the development of Kannada. Source: M.K. Dhavilkar, Kailasa – Stylistic Development and Chronology; Britannica The picture is Kailasanatha Temple, Ellora

hook: Ikshvakus were a short lived dynasty of Andhra and ruled from Nagarjunakonda, named after philosopher Nagarjuna. Which religion/sect did Nagarjuna follow?
explanation: Satavahana power in the Deccan collapsed by 3rd cent CE. New dynasties rose. Ikshvakus ruled the Andhra region between Godavri & Krishna. They lost power within a century to Pallavas from the South. It is unclear if they were connected to the Ikshvakus of Ayodhya and thus Sri Rama’s lineage. The appearance of names like Kosala & Ikshvaku in the Deccan in the period suggest migration from the north. The Ikshvakus ruled from Nagarjunakonda. Under their patronage it turned into a prominent university centred around Mahayana Buddhism. The town itself was named after Nagarjuna, the most famous of Mahayana philosophers, who had lived a few centuries earlier. Ikshvakus were eclectic in their patronage and the founder of the dynasty, Chamtamula even performed the traditional Vedic Ashvamedha sacrifice to announce his kingship. Source: RC Majumdar, “Ancient India.” The picture is Nagarjunakonda Ayaka pillar inscription of the time of Ishvaku ruler Vira-Purushadatta.

hook: Which dynasty built three capitals at Aihole, Badami and Pattadakal and left a legacy of magnificent temple architecture?
explanation: The Chalukyas rose to power in the region around Vatapi (near Bijapur) in Karnataka in 540CE. They quickly expanded their territory under warlike kings. The Chalukyas were grand temple builders in Indian history. Their trio of capital cities, Aihole-Vatapi (Badami) & Padatkkal in Karnataka are the cradle of Hindu temple architecture, with temples in all styles - Nagara(northern style), Dravida (southern style) & Vesara (mixed) and in all types – rock cut, brick, stone etc. Pulakesi II was their greatest ruler. He had defeated Kadambas of Banavasi, Alupas, and Gangas of Mysore. He made expeditions into the eastern Deccan, south Kosala, and Kalinga; and even won against Harshavardhana on the banks of the Narmada. Pulakesi II was eventually defeated & killed by the Pallavas in 642CE. His remarkable achievements were inscribed on the walls of a Jain temple at Aihole. It uses old Kannada script to write Sanskrit. It starts with the now familiar Jain greeting ‘Jai Jinendra.’ It then describes Pulakesi’s family tree & achievements, comparing him to Vedic gods and dating his reign from the Mahabharata war. The family tree helps in dating the Mahabharata war to 3137 BCE. The Chalukyas eventually lost to Rashtrakutas around 750CE. Source: Upinder Singh, A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India The picture is of Pattadakal Temples

hook: Just like the Ashokan pillar serves as our national emblem, a temple arch serves as the emblem of Telangana State. Which dynasty created the temple arch?
explanation: Kakatiyas were powerful rulers of eastern deccan between the 11th and 14th centuries. In the late 13th century the kingdom was ruled by a woman, Queen Rudramba. Her grandson & successor, Pratapa Rudra was a great ruler but was the last and was defeated & taken prisoner by Ulugh Khan (later the Delhi sultan Muhammed Tughlaq)._x000D_ _x000D_ The Kakatiyan capital was at Warangal. The original city had three concentric ring of walls. The innermost was 1.2 km in diameter and is called as the Warangal Fort. The innermost ring housed a Shiva temple which was demolished in invasions. Only the freestanding entrance portals (Toranas) on the four sides remain. The are about 10 mts in height. They were spared perhaps because they lacked any obvious religious themes._x000D_ _x000D_ The temple arch was incorporated into the State emblem along with Charminar immediately after the formation of Telangana State. In 2024, the new Congress Government finalized a new State emblem which replaced both the Kakatiya Arch and the Charminar with a martyr’s symbol. It was supposed to have been unveiled on Telangana State day, June 2, 2024, but was deferred._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: George Michelle, “Southern India”; RC Majumdar, “Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is the Telangana State Emblem.

hook: Ahilya was cursed and transformed after her infatuation with Indra. She attained salvation after Rama and Lakshmana meet her. What did she actually become?
explanation: In the Valmiki Ramayana Ahilya is only cursed to become invisible, feed on air and starve for food. There is no mention of her turning into a stone. Kalidasa, in Raghu-vamsa first describes her as having turned to stone. This in fact suggests that she remained introspective and unaffected by the world. In the popular culture, Ahilya was liberated when Rama’s feet touched her. It is also a belief that the dust from Rama’s feet touched and liberated her. In the Valmiki Ramayana, both Rama and Lakshmana fell at her feet and she looked like a brilliant flame enveloped by smoke. In fact, in a different episode, it is Rishi Vishwamitra who curses Rambha to turn into a stone when she tries to seduce him. The latter day variations emphasise Ahilya’s detachment from the world; and the positive effect that even a young Rama had (before he was even married to Sita) to mend a broken marriage. Picture: Ahilya painting by Raja Ravi Varma.

hook: In Sita Rama Kalyanam, which of these episodes is not in Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Sri Rama did not win over Sita in a Swayamwaram. In fact no Swayamwaram was even held. Shiva had bent the bow in rage during the destruction of Daksha’s Yajna. The bent bow was kept in the custody of Janaka’s clan. Many kings had earlier tried to lift Shiva’s bow. None could succeed. The defeated kings tried to siege Mithila and Janaka had warded them off. The story that Ravana felt insulted when Sita and her friends laughed at his failure to lift the bow is also not in Valmiki Ramayana. Janaka had vowed that he would only marry Sita to a man who would string the bow. At the invitation and with the permission of Vishwamitra, Rama lifts the bow easily, strings it and breaks it. The sound was so deafening that all except Janaka, Vishwamitra, Rama and Lakshmana fell down to the floor. Janaka then tells Vishwamitra that he would offer Sita in marriage to Rama. Picture: Rama stringing the bow by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Surpanaka was infatuated with Rama and beseeched him to take her. In what form did she approach him?
explanation: In many popular tellings, Surpanaka approaches Rama as a beautiful woman. However, in Valmiki Ramayana, she approaches Rama as a rakshasa in a ghastly form. She in fact tells Rama that she is a Rakshasi and can take any form she likes. She also describes her entire family to Rama. Further, Rama advises Lakshmana that he should never ridicule Rakshasas like her. The description that she came as a beautiful woman only serves to underline the ability of Rakshsas to change their form at will; and to resort to deceit to win over strong and Dharmic people. Picture: Lakshmana disfigures Surpanakha by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of the narrations related to Sita abduction is not there in Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Lakshamana does not draw a line in front of the hut to protect Sita. This incident is not in any other Sanskrit version of Ramayana too. The latter narratives remind us of the immense sense of duty that Lakshamana had to protect Rama; and the moral valour of Lakshamana that his mere line could stop anyone from entering the hut. In fact, in Valmiki Ramayana, Lakshmana says that he will go and look for Rama and that the deities of the forest may protect Sita. Picture: Sita is enthralled by the Golden deer, by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Ravana carries Sita in a chariot. Who pulls the chariot?
explanation: In popular narrative, Ravana carries Sita in a Pushpaka-Vimana or a flying chariot. In Valmiki Ramayana, he carries her in a magical chariot pulled by donkeys. However, Jatayu fights Ravana, smashes his chariot, slays the donkeys and kills the charioteer. Ravana then carries Sita physically and flies away to Lanka. The popular narrative that Ravana carried Sita scooped the earth around Sita and carried her is also not in the original Valmiki Ramayana. He holds her by the hair and thigh and carries her in his lap. The narrative only reminds us of the link that Sita has with her mother earth. No one would be able to separate Sita from mother earth and therefore she had to be carried along with earth. Picture: Ravana slays Jatayu by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of the following regarding Sabri's meeting with Rama and Lakshmana is not part of Valmiki Ramayana
explanation: There is no mention of Sabri biting the fruits. In popular narrative, Sabri bites the fruits as she is blind and cannot ensure their ripeness by checking the fruits. In Valmiki Ramayana, when Rama and Lakshamana reach Sabri’s ashram, she falls at their feet. She says that once she meets them, she is also destined to go to heaven. She mentions that she has collected several forest produce for them and then offers them. She also points them to Lake Pampa. She then seeks the permission of Rama and Lakshmana to leave her body. She immolates herself and resembling a flame, ascends to Swarga. The popular narrative underlines the Bhakti of Sabri and the acceptance of Rama of the acts of his true devotees. Picture: Sabri meets Rama, unknown source

hook: What did Hanuman not do in Lanka as per the Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Indrajit is sent by Ravana to capture Hanuman after Hanuman kills Ravana’s son Aksa. Hanuman is bound by the Brahmastra fired by Indrajit and is taken as a captive to Ravana. Here, popular narratives claim that Ravana did not offer a seat to Hanuman. To insult Ravana, Hanuman extended his tail and coiled it and created a chair for himself. It was higher than the seat of Ravana. This is not part of Valmiki Ramayana. Hanuman declares himself as a messenger of Rama. Ravana spares his life at the advice of Vibhishana as a messenger cannot be killed. Ravana only tries to punish Hanuman by lighting his tail on fire. Agni listens to Sita’s prayers and prevents the flames from burning Hanuman. Eventually, Hanuman ends up burning Lanka and flying back. Picture: Hanuman by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of these incidents at Rameswaram is not part of Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Rama is believed to have consecrated a Shivalinga at Rameshwaram before crossing the ocean; and in some accounts while returning with Sita. These are not part of Valmiki Ramayana. In fact, many of the accounts of Rama’s stay at Rameshwaram are not part of Valmiki Ramayana._x000D_ _x000D_ Vibhishana takes shelter from Rama while the sena is camping on the sea shore. Vibhishana advises Rama to seek the help of Samudra. King Sagara, an ancestor of Rama was instrumental in digging up the oceans. Therefore Samudra would help Rama, who is a descendant of King Sagara. The lord of the ocean (also referred to as Sagara) pleads helplessness as his Dharma is to be endless and he cannot become shallow for the sake of letting Rama cross. Sagara then advises Rama to take the help of Nala, the son of Vishwakarna to construct a bridge. Nala goes on to construct the bridge. Thus, the story of the squirrel, or the bricks floating when the name of Rama is written on them etc are not in the Valmiki Ramayana._x000D_ _x000D_ The Shiva Bhakti of Rama is communicated in these latter day sthala-puranas and other oral accounts. They have a positive impact and portray the unity of various traditions. They also reinforce that even avatara purushas should acknowledge the power of other devatas and pay salutations to them._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Rama subdues the Sagara, by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of these names is not used for Rama in Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: The name Ramachandra is not mentioned in Valmiki Ramayana, even though it is one of the most common names of Rama. The name became popular after 10th century and especially because of Ramcharitmanas of Tulsidas. Prior to 10th century, the name Ramachandra is not used to refer to Rama. The other three names are used for Rama in Valmiki Ramayana and refer to his lineage. Raghava describes someone belonging to the dynasty of Raghu. Kakustha was a king in the Ishwaku dynasty and this name is used for his descendants. Dasarathi means son of Dasaratha. Picture: Lineage of Rama, by DK Books

hook: Sravana is not mentioned by name in Valmiki Ramayana but his story is narrated. Who was Sravana?
explanation: After Dasaratha is separated from Rama, he narrates an incident to Kausalya. Before his marriage, Dasaratha had gone on a hunting expedition. Dasaratha had the ability to aim and shoot at a target just by hearing a sound. While in the forest, he heard a sound. It was the sound of a pot being filled by someone. Dasaratha mistook it for the sound of an elephant and shot an arrow. It hit a young tapasvi. The tapasvi was the only son of blind and frail parents and was taking care of them. The tapasvi died and Dasaratha admitted his sin to his parents. The grief stricken parents cursed that Dasaratha too would die from grief for a lost son. Dasaratha died after narrating this incident. Sravan is the name of the Tapasvi. The name is introduced in latter folklore. The tapasvi is not mentioned by name in Valmiki Ramayana. Picture: Sravan carries his blind and frail parents

hook: Which of the following objects of Sita does Lakshmana recognize?
explanation: While Sita was being abducted, she had noticed Sugriva and other vanaras on a mountaintop. Sita then wrapped her ornaments in her upper body cloth and dropped it among them, hoping it would guide Rama in his search. When Rama tells Sugriva about Sita’s abduction, he recollects seeing a lady being abducted and realizes it was Sita.Sugriva retrieves the jewels he had carefully stored and shows them to Rama who immediately recognizes them. Rama tells Lakshmana that these are indeed Sita’s. Lakshmana then tells Rama, in iconic words. “Naham jaanami keyure, naham janaami kundale, Nupure tvabhijanami nityam paadaabhivandanaat” It translates as “Bracelets I have not seen, earrings I have not seen, I recognize the anklets, as I bowed down to her feet everyday” This is a wonderful verse expressing Lakshmana’s respect for Sita. While this is considered as part of Valmiki Ramayana, it is not included in the critical edition published by the Oriental Institute, Baroda. The incidents upto Rama recognizing the jewelry are part of the critical edition. Picture: Sketches and Songs, Instagram

hook: Who raises suspicions about Sita's character in popular Uttara Kanda?
explanation: After Rama returns to Ayodhya and ruling from Ayodhya, he asks his friends about the views of citizens. Bhadra, who was an amuser for Rama, tells Rama about the aspersions that citizens cast on Sita. He also tells him that citizens wonder why Rama does not find this reprehensible and that they will also have to tolerate this from their wives. In popular narration, a washerman is mentioned as raising these doubts and aspersions. However, in Valmiki Ramayana it is the citizens at large who make these comments and not any washerman._x000D_ _x000D_ This incident occurs in Uttara Kanda. Most scholars agree that Uttara Kanda was a later addition. Both content and style of Uttara Kanda do not match with the other Kandas. The Critical Edition includes some portions of Bala Khanda and Uttara Khanda but many slokas have been omitted in it. This incident is included in the Critical Edition._x000D_ _x000D_ You can read more about this episode at https://www.dharmadispatch.in/culture/sita-parityaga-a-profound-analysis_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Sita in VanaVasa by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Shiva is not one among the nature deities who have primacy in Rig Veda. He is referred to in the text, but by a different name. What is he called?
explanation: Only 3 of 1028 hymns of the Rig Veda are exclusive to Rudra. They refer to his dangerous nature as the leader of Maruts and implore him not to strike with his arrows. They also emphasize his unmatched healing powers. Some scholars claim Rudra to be non-Vedic because he receives only the remainder (‘ucchista’) of the offerings to the gods. But later Vedas clearly venerate him. The Satarudriya hymn of the Yajur Veda extols him. It contains Rudra’s hundred names & forms. The Atharva Veda refers to him as Mahadeva, the ruler of all beings. The word Shiva in early Vedic texts is a general epithet for ‘auspiciousness’ in deities but starts getting synonymous with Rudra in the Brahmanas. Like for his devotees, Shiva remains an enigma for historians and Indologists._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Duality: The Essence of Shaivism,” Siddhant Kalra._x000D_ “Siva,” Peter Bisschop._x000D_ “Why Siva is the most mysterious of all Hindu gods,” Rohini Bakshi._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sara Welch, Wikimedia Commons

hook: Which of the following marks the first complete description of Shiva as supreme deity?
explanation: The Rig Veda has scant references to Rudra. The Yajur Veda extols hundred names of Rudra. The Atharva Veda calls him Mahadev. Around 400-200 BCE, the Svetasvatara Upanishad first describes him as Supreme Lord. It calls him as the cause of the cosmos and also as one who transcends it. The Mahabharata describes Shaiva ascetics. The Ramayana has many Shiva stories especially related to Ravana as his devotee. Patanjali (2nd cent BCE) refers to Shiva Bhagvata. Thus, Shiva as a Great God appears by 200BCE to 100CE._x000D_ Scholars say that as the Shaivite tradition spread, local gods of different regions got synonymous with Shiva - Khandoba, Mailara, Mallana & Mahakaal. The Puranas (3rd cent CE onwards) start describing Shiva’s family and many of the stories we are familiar with. Different sects appear that develop a philosophy of Shaivism alongwith Bhakti or tantra ways of worship – Pasupata, Saiva Siddhanta, Kashmiri Saivism, Veerashaiva etc. To this day, his devotees continue to develop the all encompassing godliness of Shiva._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is Verse 1.1 of the Svetasvatara Upanishad. It has one line in the middle with bhashya(commentary) above and below._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Duality: The Essence of Shaivism,” Siddhant Kalra._x000D_ “Siva,” Peter Bisschop._x000D_ “Why Siva is the most mysterious of all Hindu gods,” Rohini Bakshi._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sara Welch, Wikimedia Commons

hook: One of the earliest Shaivite worshippers were Pashupatas. Which of these popular tourist places is a Pashupata sect temple?
explanation: Elephanta is among the top attractions for Mumbai tourists. It is a Pashupata Shaivite cave temple. Pashupata Shaivism is considered as one of the earliest sects of Shaivism. It is believed to have been founded by Lakulisha who was born near Baroda in 1st Century CE. Inscriptions near Kandivili indicate the spread of Pashupata Shaivism in the present Mumbai region by 2nd Century CE. The Kalachuri dynasty that came to power in the 6th century CE were followers of Pashupata Shaivism. They sponsored the construction of several temples and monasteries during their period. Elephanta is the grandest among them. The Sadashiva (popularly and incorrectly called as Trimurti) is the most well known deity of Elephanta. The caves of Elephanta also contain several sculptures of the manifestations of Shiva. Gajasamhara, Ardhanariswara and Gangadhara are among them. The sculpltures capture all five principles of the Pashupata Shaivism, i.e Guru, Initiation, Illusion, Shivashakti and Sadashiva. Source: Mumbai Beyond Bombay, Suraj Pandit. Picture Credit: ASI

hook: A famous western author discovered a Shivling while scuba diving. It is now part of the ancient Triconamalee temple in Sri Lanka. Who is this author?
explanation: In 1956, Arthur C Clarke was diving off the coast of Trinconamalee in Sri Lanka. He found artefacts from the original Shiva temple of the Chola period, believed to have existed from atleast 3rd Century CE and perhaps from much earlier. He also found remnants of an underwater temple. The Shiva Lingam that he recovered is now consecrated and worshipped in the Koneswaram temple in Trinconamalee. His travel book "The Reefs of Taprobane" discusses his experience._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: "In the Footsteps of Rama," Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit:

hook: This Indus Valley seal is considered by many historians as "Proto Shiva." There are other differing interpretations. What is this seal called?
explanation: John Marshall who oversaw the excavations at Harappa and Mohenjo Daro concluded that this seal showed proto-shiva worship by Harappans. The seal represents a male in yogic posture, buffallo horn head-dress, animals around, an antelope and possibly an erect penis. There have been other challenges to this interpretation. Some have even claimed this is a goddess. The basics of Marshall's interpretations are still persuasive. The similiarities between the deity - for he seems no ordinary man - and certain attributes of the later-day Shiva remain striking. Artefacts of horned deity appear in other Indus Valley sites. This indicates that the worship of a horned deity goes back to early Harappan phase. Source: "A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India," Upinder Singh. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: This shivaliga is an archaeological find from an ancient site. Where was this found?
explanation: Among the mature Harappan antiquities recovered from Kalibangan is the Shivalinga. It is complete with the pitha or base. It is perhaps the most important antiquity from Kalibangan as it contributes to the controversial question of Shiva worship in the Indus Valley civilisation. The excavation was conducted between 1960 and 1969 by BB Lal and others. However, the official ASI report of the excavation with the photograph of the Shivalinga (Part 2) was published only in the year 2020. Prof BB Lal expresses his frustration at ASI denying him access to drawings to complete the report in 1973. While presenting the first volume in 2003, he says, "in presenting this report to readers, so many years after the excavations, we feel really small, nay, even ashamed." Source: "Kalibangan: Its Periods and Antiquities," Madhu Bala in "Indus Civilization Sites in India - New Discoveries," edited by Dilip K. Chakrabarti. "Excavations at Kalibangan, Parts I and II," ASI. Picture Credit: ASI

hook: Jyotirlingas are the most sacred of Shiva pilgrimage sites. Which of these states has the maximum Jyotirlings?
explanation: There are twelve Jyotirlngas across India. Jyotirlingas are places where Shiva appeared as an endless column of light or as Lingodbhava. Puranic stories describe how Vishnu and Brahma both tried to reach the extremes of the column of light and failed. The lingas in these places are not man made and Shiva is believed to have manifest himself. The first written reference to Jyotirlingas appears in Shiva Purana (approximately 12th CE). Maharashtra has three of them, at Triyambakeshwar, Bhimashankar and Grishneshwar near Ellora. Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat have two each. Surprisingly, Varanasi is the only Jyotirlinga in Uttar Pradesh. Kedarnath, Srisailam (Andhra), Baijnath (Jharkhand) and Rameswaram (Tamil Nadu) make up the rest of the Jyotirlingas. Assam claims Bhimashankar in their State as a Jyotirlinga. Maharashtra too claims that it hosts 5 Jyotirlingas. Source: Various Picture Credit: K Venkatachari

hook: Kankhal near Haridwar in Uttarakhand is tied to a life event of Shiva. That life event gave rise to a popular group of temples. Which is this temple group?
explanation: Kankhal is where Sati, wife of Rudra, killed herself since her father Daksha humiliated both her and Rudra. In fury, Rudra would behead Daksha, and commence the Tandava carrying Sati in his arms. To stop the destructive Tandava, Vishnu cut the body of Sati to pieces with his Sudarshan Chakra, hoping this would stop Rudra's Tandava. The body pieces of Sati fell in 51 locations across the Bharatavarsha and these are celebrated as Shakti Peethas. There are varying numbers assigned to Shakti Peethas, but 51 or 52 is commonly accepted. The site of Yagna is in Kankhal close to Haridwar in Uttarakhand. It is set on the banks of a Ganga canal. There is also a Shitala devi temple nearby. The picture is of the Daksheshwara temple in Kankhal. Source: "The Sakta Pithas," D. C Sircar Picture Credit: SR Ramanujam

hook: This quaint temple is a Vishnu temple. It is connected to a life event of Shiva. Which is this event?
explanation: Triyugi Narayan is near Kedarnath in Uttarakhand. Sati, in her next birth, is believed to have done penance to marry Shiva in Gauri Kund, which is the starting point for Kedarnath pilgrimage. They were married in Triyugi Narayan. Vishnu performed the role of the brother of Parvati in the wedding and is the presiding deity of the temple. The remnants of the celestial fire of the wedding are believed to be burning in the Havan even today. The fire has seen three Yugas and therefore the temple is called Triyugi Narayan. Source: Uttarakhand Tourism Picture Credit: SR Ramanujam

hook: Which of the following works of Kalidasa is based on a family member of Shiva?
explanation: Kalidasa (5th cent CE) wrote Kumarasambhava or the Birth of Kumara. One of the names of Kartikeya, Shiva’s elder son (considered as younger son in South India) is Kumara. The story of Kumara is first described in the Mahabharata. It narrates the story of his birth as well as his consecration as the general of the armies of the gods. He is more associated with the defeat of Taraka and many more. Kalidasa borrows the birth story and makes it an epic poem. Kumarasambhava is a rich source of early Shaivism in its own right._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Siva,” Peter Bisschop._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit:

hook: Which community of Shiva worshippers wears a Linga around their neck?
explanation: Lingayats are a large Shaiva sect in Karnataka and also have a notable presence in Maharashtra. The sect was founded in the 12th century by Basavanna. The term Veerasaiva is used for Lingayats. It denotes a time when they had to defend their faith with valour._x000D_ _x000D_ They wear Linga stones obtained from the Pathalganga river near Srisailam around their neck. Lingayats wore the Linga in open defiance of Jains who opposed them. These lingas came to be called pranalingas since they defended the faith with their breath. The Lingas are preserved in little caskets made of wood or silver. They are commonly called Ishtalinga now._x000D_ _x000D_ Srisailam is the Jyothirlinga of Lord Mallikarjuna and the Shakti Peeth of Goddess Bharamaramba. Srisailam is also one of the five principal mathas of the Lingayats._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: "Temples and Legends of Andhra Pradesh,"N. Ramesan._x000D_ "The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths," Roshen Dalal_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Outlook India

hook: Which manifestation of Shiva combines both Shiva and Shakti?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Ardhanariswara represents the blending of force and grace that contributes to the creation of life. They are complementary and not contradictory._x000D_ _x000D_ Once the sage Bhringi was very devoted to worshipping Shiva. He was so devoted that he refused to worship Shakti. Shiva wanted to correct his views and show that both him and Shakti are one only. Shiva shared his body with Shakti. The sage now realized that Shiva and Shakti are one only._x000D_ _x000D_ This beautiful bronze deity is in Chennai Egmore museum._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: "Manifestations of Shiva," K. Venkatachari_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: K Venkatachari_x000D_ SR Ramanujam

hook: Kailash and Amarnath Yatras are well known Shiva pilgrimages. Shrikand Mahadev too reaches a staggering height of 5227 mts. Where is this Yatra?
explanation: The Shrikand Mahadev yatra is in Himachal and starts near Nirmand, which is on the Sutlej river. It reaches a staggering height of 5227 mts. Source: Himachal Pradesh Tourism Picture Credit: Zee News

hook: Chidambaram in Tamil Nadu is a famous Shiva temple. It is one of a group of five temples, each of which represents one element of nature. Which element does Chidambaram represent?
explanation: Chidambaram represents space or Akasa. The other four temples in this group are Kancheepuram, Thiruvannamalai and Thiruvanaikovil (Tamil Nadu) and Sri Kalahasti (Andhra Pradesh). They represent Earth, Fire, Water and Air respectively. Collectively they are called Pancha Bhootha Sthalas._x000D_ _x000D_ Nataraja is the manifestation of Shiva depicting the cosmic dance of destruction and creation. The temple contains the deity of Nataraja in the dancing form. To the right of Nataraja is the Akasa Linga or space that is invisible. It is separated by a curtain that is opened for viewing. Shiva and Parvati are believed to have emerged from this space. It is also supposed to represent the creation out of nothing._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: "Immortal India, Volume I," J.H. Dave._x000D_ "Chidambaram and Nataraja," B.G. L Swamy_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: SR Ramanujam

hook: Chakia Purva, outside Ayodhya, is one of the important places in Ram's journey. What is its significance?
explanation: Chakia Purva is a village where Rama camped on his first night outside Ayodhya in exile. The villagers of Ayodhya were determined to follow him. Before Sunrise Rama left quietly leaving false tracks in a different direction. That is why the place is called Chakia, referring to chakia diya, i.e he misguided them. The village is to the east (Purva) of the campsite. Source Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni. Picture Credit

hook: Rajapur village near Chitrakoot has an important connection with Ramayana storytelling. What is its significance?
explanation: Rajapur is the birthplace of Tulsidas. He started writing Ramacharitramanas in the year 1574 AD. Portions of the original manuscript are preserved in the home of descendants of Tulsidas' disciples._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Chitrakoot, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Chitrakoot, UP Tourism

hook: Prayagraj is one of the sites of Rama's journey. It is also a location of Kumbh Mela. Another site along Rama's journey also hosts a Kumbh Mela. Which one is it?
explanation: Nashik is one of the four locations of Kumbh Mela. It is also the site of the disfiguring of Surpanakha and the site of Sita's abduction._x000D_ _x000D_ Haridwar and Ujjain too host a Kumbh Mela. Kumbakonam in Tamil Nadu hosts a Kumbh like festival every 12 years. However, none of these towns are part of Ramayana milestones._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ https //indianculture.gov.in/intangible-cultural-heritage/social-practices-rituals-and-festive-events/kumbh-mela

hook: One of the major cities of India has a Linga made of sand. It is also believed to be the location which Rama and Lakshmana visited while searching for Sita. Which is this town?
explanation: _x000D_ Walkeshwar or Walukeshwar refers to a lingam made of Walu or sand. At Walukeshwar Rama shot an arrow into the ground which created a water tank. Thereafter, Rama scooped some sand and made a linga which has hardened over the years. Walkeshwar is in Malabar Hills in Mumbai adjacent to the Governor's Bungalow. It is a quaint location in an otherwise modern residential area._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.edwinlordweeks.org.The-Temple-And-Tank-Of-Walkeschwar-At-Bombay

hook: What is the form in which Yedeshwari, who is a part of Ramayana, is worshipped in ghatshila, near Sholapur in Maharashtra?
explanation: _x000D_ Parvati appeared before Rama in the form of Sita, looking to deceive him. The rock in which she stood is called ghat shila. When Rama recognized her as Parvati and prostrated before her, she instructed Rama to go further south and that he will find his objective. The rock in which she stood is worshipped as the deity._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ www.facebook.comShreeYedeshwariDeviYermala

hook: Suribhan is a small town near Belgavi and Hubbali in Karnataka and has a prominent place in Ramayana. How is it connected to Ramayana?
explanation: Shabri ran away from her wedding to save the lives of several animals which were earmarked for sacrifice during her wedding. She took shelter in Pampa Sarovar. Matang Rishi was using the Sarovar till then. His disciples were not comfortable with Shabri, a woman, sharing the area with monastic disciples. However Matang Rishi realized that Shabri has been blessed by Vishnu himself. He requested her to move to Suriban (Surevan) and promised that Vishnu in the avatar of Rama would visit her and share her food. He also gave her the important task of pointing Rama to Pampa Sarovar where he would find Sugreeva._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ karnatakatravel.blogspot.com201201shabari-kolla

hook: Which of the following places is associated with Kishkintha?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Hampi is believed to be the capital city of Kishkintha from where Vali and Sugreev ruled. Hampi has several temples associated with the Ramayana epic and was the capital of the Vijayanagara empire._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ www.karnatakatourism.org

hook: Reclining deities of Rama are not common. Rama is normally shown in standing posture. One of the following neighborhoods hosts a temple with a reclining Rama deity. Which is it?
explanation: The Adi Jagannatha temple in Tiruppullani near Rameswaram has a deity of reclining Rama. This is the place where Rama, on the advice of Vibishana, lay down on darba bed and prayed to Samudra raja to show a way towards Lanka. Thus, the deity is called Dharbhasayana Ramar._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ sadagopan.org

hook: This famous temple has 22 wells inside the temple. Devotees pour water from each of them before taking darshan of the deity. Where is this temple?
explanation: There are 22 tirthas in the form of wells within the Ramanathaswamy temple in Rameswaram. Devotees drench themselves with the water from each of the tirthas before they take darshan. Each tirtha is connected to the Ramayana Itihaasa._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramanujam SR

hook: Divirumpola is a location in Sri Lanka. it is connected with a legendary event of Ramayana. What is this event?
explanation: Divirumpola is the site where Sita met Rama after the war. This is also the place where the Agni Pareeksha of Sita was conducted. A stupa is built over the spot of Agni Pareeksha. A platform commemorates the place of Agnipareeksha. Divirumpola means oath at the rice fields. A wooden sculpture of Hanumanji carved out of a single mango tree is present here._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Ramayana in Lanka_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ www.flickr.comphotoslakpura

hook: The science fiction writer Arthur C Clarke discovered a Shiva Lingam while diving off the coast of this city. This Lingam is now worshipped in a famous temple in the city. Which is this city?
explanation: Arthur C Clarke, while diving off the coast of Trinconamalee found artefacts from the original Shiva temple of the Chola period. He also found remnants of an underwater temple. The Shiva Lingam is now consecrated and worshipped in the Koneswarama temple._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit

hook: Unawatuna (Rumasalla) is a location in Sri Lanka connected with the Ramayana itihaasa. What is the event that it is associated with?
explanation: Unawatuna is supposed to have vegetation unlike that of the rest of Sri Lanka, but resembling those of Himalayas. The rocks are also supposed to emit a peculiar sound when knocked, unlike that of other places in Sri Lanka. The entire hill is supposed to have plants with medicinal properties which are used even now for making traditional medicines. It is believed that this is the Sanjeevani mountain that was thrown away after resuscitating Lakshmana. The name Unawatuna also means "that which slipped between the fingers." There is another belief that this is one of the spots where parts of the Sanjeevani mountain fell while being carried, similiar to Mani Parvat in Ayodhya._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Ramayana in Lanka_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramayana Tirtha

hook: What is the significance of Bijethua Hanuman Mandir in Ayodhya district in the Ramayana itihaasa?
explanation: Bijethua is the place where Hanuman ji stopped to quench his thirst while on the way to bring Sanjeevini from Himalayas. In this place Hanumanji is supposed to have killed the son of Maricha, Kalnemi. He had been sent by Ravana to distract Hanumanji and ensure that he fails to bring the Sanjeevani back._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.facebook.comBijethua-Mahaveeran-149874771808344

hook: Bithoor is a city near Kanpur on the Ganges river. It has several important linkages to ancient and modern history. What is its connection to Ramayana?
explanation: Bithoor is rich in history. It is the location of the Valmiki Ashram. Luv and Kush are believed to have been born here. Historically, Bithoor is where NanaSaheb Peshwa spent time in his exile. It is the location of the start of 1857 Indian war of independence where 300 Britishers were slain. Rani Jhansi grew up in Bithoor and Tantya Tope taught her at Bithoor._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ UP A to Z, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ UP A to Z UP Tourism

hook: What is the significance of Shringaverpur in Ramayana?
explanation: Shringaverpur is situated on Allahabad-Lucknow road, said to be once the kingdom of Nishadraj (King of Boatmen). Lord Rama travelled till here in his chariot and dismissed his charioteer here. Sringaverpur appears to be the confines of civilisaion and after this point, Rama's journey was through forests and on foot. There is a platform on the bank of the river Ganga, Ramchaura, believed to be the place where Lord Ram stayed overnight while proceeding to his exile. His were washed by Nishadraj before he carried them across the river on his boat._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ UP A to Z UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ The Geography of Rama's Exile, F.E Fargiter, Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ UP A to Z UP Tourism

hook: Anandpur is one of the five Takhts of the Sikhs. Several politically significant events took place here. Takht Keshgarh Sahib in Anandpur marks a seminal event in Sikh history? Which of these events is it?
explanation: Anandpur was the site of anointment of Punj Piaray (Five Choosen Beloved Ones). They volunteered to be martyrs when Guru Gobind Singh wanted to test the congregation. They were later on administered amrit by Guru Gobind Singh and declared as Khalsas. The Khara Prasad is always offered to the Panja Pyaras first. Guru Gobind Singh also founded the Khalsa Panth in Anandpur. In the year 1689, Rani Champa of Bilaspur (present day Himachal Pradesh) requested Guru Gobind Singh to establish a new town as the capital of the Sikh state and also to build forts for the defence of the State. Guru Gobind Singh acceded to the request and built a new town and five forts in the vicinity. In modern history, Anandpur is also the site of the Anandpur Sahib resolution of the Shironmani Akali Dal in 1973 which led to the political movement in Punjab. Anandpur also celebrates Hola Mohalla every year around Holi. Around 20 lakh people gather for the celebration which also marks the congregation of Nihangs from all over India. Takht Kesgarh Saheb is one of the five Takhts or seats of authority in Sikhism. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir Anandpur Sahib, Harjinder Singh Dilgeer Picture Credit: Navtejpvs Flickr Sikh Inside Facebook

hook: Rewalsar is on a picturesque mountain top and on the banks of a lake. Guru Gobind Singh united several hill rulers against a common enemy at this site. The site is also sacred to another religion. Which religion is it?
explanation: Rewalsar is the location of the conference of hill rulers of present day Himachal Pradesh. They feared the Mughal army. At their request Guru Gobind Singh presided over a conference of the hill rulers in Rewalsar. He extracted a promise from them that they would stay united and would not accept supremacy of Mughal rulers in any manner._x000D_ _x000D_ Buddhists venerate Rewalsar as the location where he emerged unharmed from an immolation attempt. The daughter of a king had fallen in love with Padmasambhava. The enraged king attempted to burn both Padamashabhava and his daughter alive, but the pyre had turned into a lake from which both emerged alive seated on a lotus. Padmasambhava is believed to have flown from Rewalsar lake to Tibet._x000D_ _x000D_ Rewalsar is also the location where Rishi Lomas, who is mentioned in Skanda Purana, meditated on Shiva._x000D_ _x000D_ Two small grassy islands float within the lake and these are considered to be divine manifestations._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Anandpur Sahib, Harjinder Singh Dilgeer_x000D_ Buddhist Monasteries of Himachal, OC Handa_x000D_ Mandi Gazatteer, 1904_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: RTPR9 Wikimedia Commons

hook: This Gurudwara was once a residence. It marked the stay of Guru Sri Harikishan when he visited Delhi. Which Gurudwara is this?
explanation: Bangla Sahib was once the property of Raja Jai Singh of Amber (Jaipur) where Guru Harikishan stayed._x000D_ _x000D_ Guru Sri Harkishan was appointed as the eight Guru even though he was only five years old. His detaractors including his elder brother complained to Aurangazeb who summoned Guru Harkishan to Delhi. Raja Jai Singh took the responsibility of persuading Sri Harkishan to Delhi. He also got an assurance from Aurangzeb that the Guru would stay with him in his bungalow till the succession issue was satisfactorily addressed._x000D_ _x000D_ Guru Harkishan spent most of his time in Delhi serving the sick and destitute and won many admirers. He healed people who were afflicted with small pox during that time, but he himself succumbed to the infection when he was only ten years old._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Nimit Nigam Wikimedia Commons

hook: This lakeside Gurudwara is one of the holiest pilgrimages of Sikhism and is accessible only through a trekking trail. Where is this?
explanation: Hemkund is a special Gurudwara. This is the place where Guru Gobind Singh is supposed to have medidated in his earlier life. In his own words he says "I was busy performing penance on the hills of Hemkunt where seven peaks are prominent. The place is called Sapt Shring where King Pandu had performed Yoga." The location was identified only in 1930s._x000D_ _x000D_ Hemkund is in the Garhwal Himalayas and adjoins the Valley of Flowers which is a very popular hiking and nature destination. Hemkund is accessible only by a steep hiking trail. The roadhead from Haridwar to Badrinath branches off towards the Hemkund hiking trail after Joshimath._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Hemkund Sahib Gurudwara Facebook

hook: This Gurudwara is the Manikaran Sahib in the Parvati valley. It is very close to Kullu Himachal Pradesh. It is famous for a natural phenomenon. What is it?
explanation: Manikaran Sahib is on the banks of the Parvati river. It is famous for its hot springs. Guru Nanak ji visited this region in the 16th century. When his followers were hungry, Guru Nanak ji asked the villagers for support. While the villagers donated cooking ingredients, there was no fire. Guru Nanak ji lifted a stone and a hot water spring appeared. It was hot enough to cook the food. A Gurudwara has been built in this spot. Even today food is cooked in the hot springs. The hot springs are also believed to have curative powers and many pilgrims brave the heat to benefit from the medicinal properties._x000D_ _x000D_ Manikaran is also sacred for Hindus. They believe the jewels of Parvati were stolen by a serpent while she was bathing in the water of the Parvati river. The name Manikaran is a derivate of Mani (jewel) and Karan(ear). Manikaran also houses a famous Ram temple whose deity is believed to have been brought from Ayodhya. The deity was later on shifted to Kullu._x000D_ _x000D_ The Parvati valley where Manikaran is located is also famous for Malana village, whose residents are believed to be the soldiers left behind from Alexandar's army. Kasol is a nearby backpacking destination._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sikhwiki.org_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Adarsh Kuruvath Flickr

hook: Gurudwara Hazur Saheb marks the spot where Guru Gobind Singh breathed his last. This Gurudwara is on the banks of a famous river of India. Which State is it in?
explanation: Nanded in Maharashtra is the location where Guru Gobind Singh spent his last days in the year 1708. Before his death, the Guru had declared, “All my Sikhs are hereby ordered to accept the Granth as their Preceptor. Have faith in the holy Granth as your master and consider it the visible manifestation of the Gurus.” More than a hundred years later, Maharaja Ranjit Singh ordered a Gurudwara to be built on the banks of the river Godavari. The personal belongings of Guru Gobind Singh including swords and bow & arrows are preserved in the Gurudwara. It is one of the five Takhts of Sikhism. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir Picture Credit: Sandeep Chetan Flickr

hook: This Gurudwara is across the Ravi river in Pakistan. Recently the Governments of India and Pakistan constructed an exclusive access corridor so that Sikhs from India can visit the Gurudwara without a visa. Which is this Gurudwara?
explanation: Kartarpur corridor connects Dera Baba Nanak which is a km away from the border on the Indian side to Darbar Sahib in Kartarpur, Pakistan which is about 5 km from the border. Both these Gurudwaras have an intimate connection with the life of Guru Nanak. Dera Baba Nanak is on the Indian side of river Ravi on the Indo Pak border. Guru Nanak used to come here for meditation during the last days of his life. He also established Kartarpur town across the river and breathed his last at the spot marked by Kartarpur Sahib. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir Picture Credit: Nina Robinson Flickr

hook: This Gurudwara, Nanak Jhira, is in a region outside the popular influence of Sikhism. However the place made an important contribution to Sikhism. Where is this Gurudwara?
explanation: Guru Nanak travelled all over India and outside India covering Assam, Sri Lanka, Tibet and Mecca. During his second Udasi or Missionary tour of South India, he travelled to Nanded, Hyderabad, Golconda and Bidar. In Bidar he met Pir Jalaluddin and Yakoob Ali. The Guru accompanied by his disciple Mardana stayed in the outskirts of Bidar where the Nanak Jhira Gurudwara is now located. The people in the region came to visit him in large numbers. The region suffered from acute water scarcity and Guru Nanak is believed to have touched the hillside with his toe and had removed some rubble which revealed the source of a spring water. The place is now called Nanak Jhira. Eventually Bidar would also find an important place in Sikh history. One of the Panj Piaras anointed by Guru Gobind Singh would hail from Bidar. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir Picture Credit: Baljinder Kang Wikimedia Commons

hook: Harmandir Sahib is the Gurudwara in Golden Temple Amritsar. There is another Sri Harmandir Ji on the banks of a famous river in India. Guru Gobind Singh was born here. In which town is this Gurudwara?
explanation: Guru Gobind Singh was born in December 1666 at Patna. The site of the Gurudwara was originally the haveli of Salis Rai Johri who was a great devotee of Guru Nanak. He converted his palatial house into a dharamsala. When Guru Tegh Bahadur, Guru Gobind’s father visited Patna, he stayed here. Later it became a gurudwara._x000D_ _x000D_ The building was destroyed by fire and Maharaja Ranjit Singh reconstructed it in 1839. It was again destroyed in the devastating Bihar earthquake of 1934 and built again in the 1950s._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ashish Agarwal Flickr

hook: The Gurudwara marks the birthplace of the founder of Sikhism, Guru Nanak Dev Ji. What is this Gurudwara called?
explanation: Gurudwara Nankana Sahib was built around 1600 CE and renovated under Maharaja Randhir Singh in 1819-20. It is about 90 kms west of Lahore in Pakistan._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: This Gurudwara in Delhi marks the spot where Guru Tegh Bahadur was executed on the orders of Emperor Aurangzeb. What is its name?
explanation: Guru Tegh Bahadur was executed on the orders of Emperor Aurangzeb in 1675. He paid the price for espousing the cause of the freedom of worship of Kashmiri Pandits against whom the emperor had unleashed atrocities. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir Picture Credit: Den.be Flickr

hook: A famous medieval sun temple, in present day Pakistan, was among the first temples to be sacked by Arab invaders. It was destroyed by Muhammed bin Qasim who led the expedition to capture Sindh between 708-11 CE. In which city was it located?
explanation: Hiuen Tsang, the Chinese traveller who came to India in the 7th century CE refers to the Multan temple with a golden deity. It was first sacked by bin Qasim. The devotees rebuilt the temple with a different idol made of wood and covered by red Cordovan leather. The popularity of the temple was such that the Muslim rulers used the idol as a hostage to deter any attempts to reconquer Multan. A Persian chronicler Istakhari noted in 951CE, "when the Indians make war upon them and endeavour to seize the idol, the inhabitants bring it out, pretending that they will break it and burn it. Upon this the Indians retire." The temple was destroyed and rebuilt a few more times being finally destroyed in the reign of Aurangzeb. The site is now lost._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is that of devotees at Sunrise in Kanyakumari, the southern tip of the mainland. A structure can disappear, but the faith stays._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Flight of Deities & Rebirth of Temples. Meenakshi Jain._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R Ramanujam

hook: One of the famous pilgrimage sites of India also was the location of 14 Sun temples. These were razed after 12th century but remnants of them still are worshipped. Which pilgrimage site is this?
explanation: The first sections of the Kashi Khanda list 12 adityas in Varanasi. Later chapters give descriptions of two more adityas (Sumantvaditya and Karnaditya), bringing the number of temples and shrines up to fourteen. Each of the original fourteen aditya temples of Varanasi was razed by invaders. However, the adityas remained in the communal memory of the city. Today, the fourteen adityas are marked by a variety of small and unpretentious features such as carved Sun disks, lotus-form stones or images of Surya, which are set into walls, installed in small shrines or the precincts of temples._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is of Lolarka Kunda in Varanasi, which has an image of trembling Sun._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Malville, J. McKim and Singh, Rana P.B. 1995 i. Visual astronomy in the mythology and ritual of India: The Sun temples of Varanasi._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Rana P.B. Singh

hook: The Hindi film Haidar had a song filmed at the ruins of a sun temple built by the great Kashmiri king Lalitaditya Mukatapida of the Karakota dynasty. He ruled in the first half of the 8th century CE. He is sometimes described as a world conqueror having defeated kings from Central Asia to Bengal and from Kanauj to the Deccan. What is name of this temple?
explanation: Martand was a famous Sun temple. Legend connects the spring of Martand tirtha with the production of the Sun from a lifeless egg (mrtanda). The temple was destroyed by the Kashmiri king Sikander Shah (1389-1413CE) called Butsikhan (Iconoclast).
